title: Dyeing with logwood chips
subtitle: Pre-soaking and simmering Haematoxylum campechianum heartwood chips to dye alum-mordanted wool a deep purple
excerpt: Logwood heartwood chips give alum-mordanted wool a deep purple, one of the most intense colours available from a natural dye at relatively low material weight. With an iron modifier the colour moves to near-black; with copper it shifts to a navy-grey. The chips require an overnight soak before extracting the dye bath. This tutorial covers the full method from pre-soaking through to wastewater disposal.
sourceNotes:
Hummel, J. J. The Dyeing of Textile Fabrics. London: Cassell, 1885. Public domain. Internet Archive. Logwood mordant response tables and dye-bath handling notes.

Pellew, Charles Livingston. Dyes and Dyeing. New York, 1913. Public domain. Internet Archive. Logwood colour range by mordant.

glossaryTerms.definition: The heartwood of Haematoxylum campechianum, a tree native to Central America and the Caribbean. Logwood has been imported into Europe as a dye material since the sixteenth century. The active pigment is haematoxylin, which gives purple to black shades on mordanted wool and silk. Logwood chips are sold by natural-dye suppliers in the UK and require a short pre-soak before extraction.

A natural dye material sourced from outside the grower's country rather than grown or gathered locally. Logwood, madder, weld, and indigo are all commonly imported as dried or chipped material. Imported dyes are still considered natural dyes; the distinction matters for sourcing provenance and traceability rather than for the dye chemistry.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Dye-pots are dedicated and never return to food use. Wear gloves, an apron, and closed-toe shoes. Mordant work goes outdoors or with extraction.

content.text: from the heartwood of Haematoxylum campechianum, a Central American tree used for dyeing since the sixteenth century.

chips give alum-mordanted wool a deep purple at a low material ratio of 25% weight of fibre (WOF). The colour shifts to a near-black with an iron modifier and to a navy-grey with copper. The chips need an overnight soak before extraction to soften the wood and release the pigment. Logwood overdyes deeply: use precise weights and remove the wool promptly once the depth wanted is reached.

items.name: Wool yarn or fleece, pre-wetted in cool water for 30 minutes
Logwood chips (25% WOF), soaked overnight in cold water
Stainless steel dye-pot, dedicated to dyeing
Fine mesh sieve or muslin cloth for straining
Kilner jar or container for overnight chip soak

content.text: Place 25 g of logwood chips in a kilner jar or container and cover with cold water. Leave to soak overnight, at least 8 to 12 hours. The soaking water will turn a deep red-purple as the haematoxylin starts to dissolve out of the wood. Do not discard this water: transfer chips and soaking liquid together into the dye-pot.

Dissolve 25 g of alum potash in 200 ml of hot water in the mordant jar and stir into a separate pot of warm water large enough for the wool to move freely. Lower the pre-wetted wool into the mordant bath. Bring slowly to 80 degrees C over 30 minutes and hold for 45 minutes. Turn every 10 minutes. Remove the wool, squeeze gently without wringing, and set aside without rinsing. The mordant step can run in parallel with the chip soak if timed correctly.

Transfer the soaked chips and their liquid to the dye-pot and top up with enough water to allow the wool to move freely. Bring to 80 degrees C and hold for 30 minutes, pressing the chips occasionally to encourage pigment release. Strain the bath through a fine mesh sieve, pressing the chips firmly. Discard the spent chips and return the deep red-purple liquid to the pot.

Lower the mordanted wool into the dye-bath. Bring to 80 degrees C over 20 minutes and hold for 60 minutes. Logwood colours up fast: check the colour after 30 minutes by lifting a small section and rinsing it quickly under cold water. If the deep purple is already where you want it, remove the wool early. Overdyeing is the most common error with logwood: at 25% WOF the chips hold enough pigment to saturate the fibre, and extra time in the bath shifts the result past purple toward a dark blue-brown.

To shift the deep purple toward near-black, dissolve 2 g of iron sulphate in 200 ml of water and add to the dye-bath in the final 10 minutes. Stir well and check the colour on a lifted section before committing the full skein. The shift from purple to near-black happens within 5 to 10 minutes on logwood-dyed wool. Remove the wool as soon as the colour is where you want it.

Lift the wool from the dye-bath and cool slightly before rinsing. Rinse in water at a similar temperature to avoid felting. Logwood bleeds on the first rinse: rinse until the water runs only faintly coloured. Squeeze gently without wringing and dry in shade away from direct sun. Logwood purple is reasonably fast once mordanted with alum, but fades over years in strong light.

Alum mordant (25% WOF) with logwood chips at 25% WOF gives the base deep purple: the colour logwood is most associated with in the historical dye literature. An iron modifier (2% WOF iron sulphate) added in the final 10 minutes shifts the result to near-black. A copper modifier (2% WOF copper sulphate) gives a navy-grey, cooler and less intense than the iron result.

Reducing the chip ratio to 15% WOF gives a softer grape-purple on alum wool rather than the deep saturated result. This is useful when a lighter shade is wanted without diluting the dye-bath with more water. A second exhausted dye-bath from the same chips, after the first skein has been dyed, produces a softer slate-lavender suitable for lighter accent skeins.

items.problem: The wool came out a dark blue-brown rather than purple
items.cause: Wool left in the dye-bath too long, or dye-bath temperature exceeded 85 degrees C
items.fix: Check the colour at 30 minutes by rinsing a lifted section under cold water. Logwood saturates quickly at 25% WOF. Remove the wool as soon as the deep purple is reached.

items.problem: The colour is uneven with dark patches
items.cause: Wool was not pre-wetted before the dye-bath, so dry fibres grabbed colour faster where they touched the liquid first
items.fix: Pre-wet the wool for at least 30 minutes in cool water before the dye-bath and lower it into the warm bath gently. Turn every 10 minutes throughout the dye time.

items.problem: Very heavy bleeding in the rinse water, colour loss after drying
items.cause: Mordant was insufficient or the dye-bath temperature was too low during dyeing
items.fix: Ensure the mordant bath reached 80 degrees C and was held for the full 45 minutes. A logwood dye-bath that never reaches 75 to 80 degrees C may deposit surface colour that washes out rather than bonding to the mordanted fibre.

content.text: A plain alum and logwood dye-bath is low-hazard at home quantities. Once cooled, it can go down a foul drain. If an iron modifier was used, the spent bath must go to a foul drain only: not a storm drain, stream, soak-away, or compost heap. Iron sulphate is harmful to aquatic invertebrates even in small concentrations and must not enter any watercourse. For quantities larger than approximately 2 litres of iron modifier bath, contact your local council waste service before disposal. Copper sulphate modifier baths carry the same restriction as iron: foul drain only, never a storm drain or watercourse.
